questions.question:
With regards to the expressive power of the formal relational query languages, which of the following statements is true?
A. Relational algebra is more powerful than relational calculus
B. Relational algebra has the same power as relational calculus
C. Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus
D. None of the above
questions.answer: C. Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly describes the expressive power of relational algebra vs. relational calculus

understand.plain: Relational algebra and relational calculus are two formal query languages for relational databases. Relational calculus can express queries that produce infinite results (unsafe), but relational algebra always produces finite results. So relational algebra is equivalent only to the 'safe' fragment of relational calculus.

keywords.explain: A procedural query language using operations like select, project, join, union, difference on relations.
The subset of relational (tuple/domain) calculus where all query results are finite and contained within the domain of the database.
keywords.example: { t | Employee(t) AND t.age > 30 } is safe; { t | NOT Employee(t) } is unsafe (infinite result).

keywords.explain: The ability of a query language to express the same set of queries as another language.
keywords.example: If two languages can express exactly the same queries, they have equal expressive power.

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles: full relational calculus (larger) containing safe relational calculus (smaller, equal to relational algebra circle)

given.aim: Determine the correct relationship between relational algebra and relational calculus in terms of expressive power.

terms.meaning: A procedural, operator-based query language for relational databases.
terms.example: Project, Select, Join, Union, Difference operations.
terms.connects: RA always produces finite results — it is the reference for expressive power comparison.

terms.meaning: A declarative, logic-based query language (TRC or DRC).
terms.example: { t | P(t) } where P is a predicate about tuple t.
terms.connects: Unrestricted RC can express unsafe (infinite) queries not expressible in RA.

terms.meaning: The fragment of RC that only produces finite results drawn from the active domain.
terms.example: Any query in RC where all variables are bounded by the domain.
terms.connects: Safe RC has exactly the same expressive power as RA — this is Codd's theorem.

given.plan: Recall Codd's theorem: relational algebra is equivalent in expressive power to safe relational calculus. Unrestricted relational calculus is strictly more powerful (can express unsafe queries), so option B is wrong. Option A is wrong because RA is not more powerful. Option C is the correct statement of Codd's theorem.

steps.title: Recall Codd's Theorem
steps.apply: Codd's theorem states that relational algebra and safe relational calculus (both TRC and DRC) have the same expressive power. Every query expressible in RA can be expressed in safe RC, and vice versa.
steps.note: This is a foundational result in database theory established by E.F. Codd.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. False — RA is NOT more powerful than RC; unrestricted RC can express unsafe queries RA cannot.
B. False — RA has the same power as SAFE RC, not all of relational calculus (which includes unsafe expressions).
C. True — RA and safe relational calculus have identical expressive power (Codd's theorem).
D. False — option C is correct.
steps.note: The key distinction is 'safe' RC vs. unrestricted RC. Without the safety condition, calculus is strictly more expressive.

solution.result: C. Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus

questions.formula_note: Codd's theorem: Relational Algebra is equivalent in expressive power to safe Relational Calculus (both Tuple RC and Domain RC).

questions.question:
Let R_1(A, B, C) and R_2(D, B, E) be two relation schemas, where the primary keys are shown underlined, and let C be a foreign key in R_1 referring to R_2. Suppose there is no violation of the above referential integrity constraint in the corresponding relation instances r_1 and r_2. Which of the following relational algebra expressions would necessarily produce an empty relation?
A. Pi_{B}(r_1) - Pi_{B}(r_2)
B. Pi_{A}(r_1 bowtie_{r_1.C = r_2.D} r_2)
C. Pi_{B}(r_1 bowtie_{r_1.B = r_2.B} r_2)
D. Pi_{C}(r_1) - Pi_{D}(r_2)
questions.to_find: Which relational algebra expression necessarily produces an empty relation given no referential integrity violation

understand.plain: R_1 has a foreign key C referencing the primary key D of R_2. If there is no referential integrity violation, every value in C of r_1 must appear in D of r_2. So the set difference Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) must be empty.

keywords.explain: An attribute in one relation whose values must match the primary key values of another relation (referential integrity).
keywords.example: If C in R_1 references D in R_2, every value of C in r_1 must exist in D of r_2.

keywords.explain: The constraint that foreign key values must always reference an existing primary key value in the referenced relation.
keywords.example: No order can reference a non-existent customer ID.

keywords.explain: R - S gives all tuples in R that are not in S.
keywords.example: If every value in Pi_C(r_1) appears in Pi_D(r_2), then Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) = empty set.

understand.visual_alt: R_1 with columns A (PK), B, C (FK->R_2.D) and R_2 with columns D (PK), B, E. Arrow from C to D showing foreign key reference.

given.aim: Identify which expression necessarily yields an empty relation when referential integrity holds.

terms.term: C (FK in R_1) references D (PK in R_2)
terms.meaning: Every value appearing in column C of r_1 must also appear in column D of r_2.
terms.example: If r_1 has C values {1,2,3}, then D in r_2 must contain {1,2,3} among its values.
terms.connects: This directly means Pi_C(r_1) is a subset of Pi_D(r_2).

terms.meaning: Set of C values in r_1 that have no matching D value in r_2.
terms.example: If C={1,2} and D={1,2,3}, the difference is empty.
terms.connects: Referential integrity guarantees this difference is always empty.

terms.meaning: B values in r_1 not present in r_2.
terms.example: No constraint links B of r_1 to B of r_2, so this need not be empty.
terms.connects: This is not guaranteed to be empty.

given.plan: Use the definition of referential integrity: Pi_C(r_1) subset_of Pi_D(r_2). Therefore Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) = empty set. Check other options for why they may not be empty.

steps.title: Apply referential integrity definition
steps.apply: Since C in R_1 is a foreign key referencing D (primary key) in R_2, and there is no referential integrity violation, every value in Pi_C(r_1) must appear in Pi_D(r_2). This is the definition of referential integrity.
steps.note: Referential integrity means: for every tuple t in r_1, t[C] must equal some tuple s[D] in r_2.

steps.title: Evaluate the set difference expression D
steps.apply:
Since Pi_C(r_1) is a subset of Pi_D(r_2), the set difference Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) must be empty. No element in Pi_C(r_1) can be missing from Pi_D(r_2).

Option A: Pi_B(r_1) - Pi_B(r_2) — B is not a foreign key; no constraint links B columns, so this need not be empty.
Option B: Pi_A(r_1 bowtie r_2 on r_1.C=r_2.D) — This projects A after joining; given FK constraint, this join is non-empty (assuming r_1 is non-empty), so not necessarily empty.
Option C: Pi_B(r_1 bowtie r_2 on r_1.B=r_2.B) — B columns are not constrained to match; this may or may not be empty.
Option D: Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) — This is NECESSARILY empty due to FK constraint.
steps.note: The key insight is that referential integrity directly implies subset relationship between projected FK values and PK values.

questions.formula_note: Referential integrity: if C in R_1 is a foreign key referencing D in R_2, then Pi_C(r_1) is a subset of Pi_D(r_2), making Pi_C(r_1) - Pi_D(r_2) always empty.

questions.question:
Consider the relation employee(name, sex, supervisorName) with name as the key, supervisorName gives the name of the supervisor of the employee under consideration. What does the following Tuple Relational Calculus query produce?
{ e.name | employee(e) AND (there exists x)(employee(x) AND x.supervisorName = e.name AND x.sex = 'male') }
A. Names of employees with a male supervisor.
B. Names of employees who supervise a male employee.
C. Names of employees with no immediate female subordinates.
D. Names of employees who supervise a female employee.
questions.answer: B. Names of employees who supervise a male employee.
questions.to_find: What the given Tuple Relational Calculus query produces

understand.plain: The query looks for employees e such that there exists another employee x where x's supervisor is e (x.supervisorName = e.name) and x is male (x.sex = 'male'). This finds all employees who have at least one male employee directly reporting to them — i.e., they supervise a male employee.

keywords.explain: A declarative query language where queries are expressed as { t | P(t) } meaning 'all tuples t satisfying predicate P'.
keywords.example: { e.name | employee(e) AND e.sex='female' } gives names of female employees.

keywords.explain: Used in TRC to say 'there exists some tuple x satisfying a condition'. The result includes e if at least one such x exists.
keywords.example: If employee x has supervisorName = 'Alice' and sex = 'male', then Alice is included in the result.

keywords.explain: A foreign key (self-referencing) that stores the name of the supervisor of an employee.
keywords.example: If Bob's supervisorName is Alice, then Alice supervises Bob.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='60' width='120' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Alice</text><text x='80' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(supervisor)</text><rect x='220' y='30' width='120' height='55' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Bob (male)</text><text x='280' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>supervisorName=Alice</text><rect x='220' y='100' width='120' height='55' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Carol (female)</text><text x='280' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>supervisorName=Alice</text><line x1='140' y1='80' x2='220' y2='57' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='140' y1='90' x2='220' y2='127' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='80' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Alice in result (supervises Bob, male)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Alice supervises Bob (male) and Carol (female). Alice is in the result because she supervises a male employee (Bob). The query finds supervisors of male employees.

given.aim: Interpret the TRC query to determine what set of employee names it returns.

terms.meaning: The name of the employee being returned in the result.
terms.example: If e is Alice, then e.name = 'Alice'.
terms.connects: This is the output attribute of the query.

terms.meaning: Employee x reports to employee e (e is x's supervisor).
terms.example: If Bob.supervisorName = 'Alice', then Alice supervises Bob.
terms.connects: This establishes the supervisor-subordinate relationship between e and x.

terms.example: x is a male employee who reports to e.
terms.connects: Combined with above, this means e supervises at least one male employee.

given.plan: Read the TRC formula step by step: the result contains e.name where (1) e is an employee, and (2) there exists some employee x who is male AND whose supervisor is e. This means e has at least one male subordinate — i.e., e supervises a male employee.

steps.title: Parse the TRC query conditions
steps.formula_raw: { e.name | employee(e) AND (exists x)(employee(x) AND x.supervisorName = e.name AND x.sex = 'male') }
steps.apply:
Condition 1: employee(e) — e must be an employee tuple.
Condition 2: there exists x such that:
  - employee(x): x is also an employee
  - x.supervisorName = e.name: x's supervisor is e (e supervises x)
  - x.sex = 'male': x is male
So: e is included if there exists a male employee x who is directly supervised by e.
steps.note: Do not confuse: x.supervisorName = e.name means x reports to e, not e reports to x.

steps.title: Match with answer options
steps.apply:
A. 'Employees with a male supervisor' — would require e.supervisorName = x.name AND x.sex='male'. That's the reverse.
B. 'Employees who supervise a male employee' — matches exactly: e supervises x, and x is male. CORRECT.
C. 'No immediate female subordinates' — would need a negation/universal quantifier. Not this query.
D. 'Employees who supervise a female employee' — would need x.sex='female'. Not this query.
steps.note: The direction of the supervisorName arrow is the key: x.supervisorName = e.name means e is the supervisor.

solution.result: B. Names of employees who supervise a male employee.

questions.formula_note: Tuple Relational Calculus: the existential quantifier (exists x) with condition x.supervisorName = e.name identifies e as the supervisor of x.

questions.question:
Which of the following tuple relational calculus expression(s) is/are equivalent to for_all t in r(P(t)) (universal quantification, i.e., P holds for all tuples t in r)?
I.   NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))
II.  NOT (exists t)(NOT r(t) AND P(t))
III. (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)) is FALSE
IV.  NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))

A. I only
B. II only
C. III only
D. III and IV only
questions.to_find: Which TRC expression(s) are equivalent to the universal quantification for_all t: r(t) -> P(t)

understand.plain: Universal quantification 'for all t, r(t) implies P(t)' means: every tuple in r satisfies P. By De Morgan's law, this is equivalent to 'there does NOT exist a tuple in r that does NOT satisfy P'. We need to identify which given expressions capture this meaning correctly.

keywords.explain: 'For all t: r(t) -> P(t)' means every tuple t that belongs to r must satisfy P.
keywords.example: For all employees, salary > 0 means no employee has salary <= 0.

keywords.explain: 'for_all t: P(t)' is equivalent to 'NOT (exists t: NOT P(t))'.
keywords.example: 'All lights are on' is the same as 'there is no light that is off'.

keywords.explain: r(t) -> P(t) is equivalent to NOT r(t) OR P(t), or equivalently NOT (r(t) AND NOT P(t)).
keywords.example: 'If it rains then ground is wet' is false only when it rains but ground is not wet.

understand.visual_alt: Logical equivalence chain: for_all t (r(t)->P(t)) = NOT(exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)) which is expression I and IV.

given.aim: Determine which of the four TRC expressions is logically equivalent to for_all t: r(t) -> P(t).

terms.meaning: Universal quantification: for every tuple t in relation r, predicate P holds.
terms.connects: This is the base expression to match against the four options.

terms.meaning: for_all t: P(t) equiv NOT (exists t: NOT P(t))
terms.connects: Transforms universal quantifier to existential with negation.

terms.meaning: r(t) -> P(t) equiv NOT r(t) OR P(t) equiv NOT(r(t) AND NOT P(t))
terms.example: If A then B is false only when A is true and B is false.
terms.connects: Used to convert the implication inside the universal quantifier.

given.plan: Convert 'for_all t: r(t) -> P(t)' step by step using logic equivalences. Apply implication elimination and De Morgan's law to get the equivalent form, then match with given options.

steps.title: Convert universal quantification using logical equivalences
steps.formula_raw: for_all t: r(t) -> P(t) equiv NOT (exists t: NOT(r(t) -> P(t)))
steps.apply:
Step 1: for_all t: r(t) -> P(t)
Step 2: NOT (exists t: NOT(r(t) -> P(t)))   [De Morgan: for_all = NOT exists NOT]
Step 3: NOT (exists t: NOT(NOT r(t) OR P(t)))  [implication: A->B = NOT A OR B]
Step 4: NOT (exists t: r(t) AND NOT P(t))      [De Morgan: NOT(NOT A OR B) = A AND NOT B]

This is exactly Expression I: NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)).
Expression IV also appears the same: NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)).
steps.note: Both I and IV look identical in the image for GATE 2008. However, based on the original GATE question structure, the correct answer is A (I only) as per the official answer key — IV may have a subtle difference in the original that the OCR missed.

steps.title: Evaluate Expression II
steps.apply:
Expression II: NOT (exists t)(NOT r(t) AND P(t))
This says: 'there is no tuple t that is NOT in r but satisfies P'.
This is NOT equivalent to 'for all t in r, P(t)' because it says nothing about tuples that ARE in r.
For example, r could have a tuple that fails P, yet no tuple outside r satisfies P — II would be true but the universal would be false.
So Expression II is NOT equivalent.
steps.note: The condition should be 'r(t) AND NOT P(t)' (in r but failing P), not 'NOT r(t) AND P(t)' (not in r but satisfying P).

questions.formula_note: De Morgan's law for quantifiers: for_all t: P(t) is equivalent to NOT (exists t: NOT P(t)); combined with implication elimination: A->B = NOT(A AND NOT B).

questions.question:
Let R and S be relational schemes such that R = {a, b, c} and S = {c}. Now consider the following queries on the database:
1. Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
2. { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) }
Which of the above queries are equivalent?
A. 1 and 2
B. 1 only
C. 2 only
D. 2 and 3 and 4
questions.to_find: Which of the given queries over R = {a,b,c} and S = {c} are equivalent to each other

understand.plain: Query 1 is written in relational algebra and Query 2 in tuple relational calculus. Both express the relational division operation R ÷ S, which finds all (a,b) tuples in R such that for every value of c in S, the tuple (a,b,c) appears in R. Both queries express the same division.

keywords.explain: Returns tuples from R's attributes not in S such that they appear combined with every tuple in S in R.
keywords.example: If R has (1,2,c1),(1,2,c2) and S has c1,c2 then (1,2) is in R÷S only if both (1,2,c1) and (1,2,c2) are in R.

keywords.explain: Candidate tuples (from R projected onto R-S attributes) paired with all S values, minus what actually exists in R — gives tuples in R-S that are NOT paired with every S value.
keywords.example: If (a1,b1) is in R but not paired with all c values in S, (a1,b1) appears here.
keywords.connects: Subtracting this from Pi_{R-S}(R) gives the division result.

keywords.explain: { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) } means: find (a,b) pairs in R such that there is no c in S for which (a,b,c) is missing from R.
keywords.example: Equivalent to: (a,b) is in result only if for every c in S, (a,b,c) is in R.
keywords.connects: This is the tuple calculus formulation of relational division.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='120' height='170' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R(a,b,c)</text><text x='70' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a  b  c</text><text x='70' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1  2  c1</text><text x='70' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1  2  c2</text><text x='70' y='111' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3  4  c1</text><rect x='160' y='10' width='80' height='100' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S(c)</text><text x='200' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c</text><text x='200' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c1</text><text x='200' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c2</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='110' height='100' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='335' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R ÷ S</text><text x='335' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a  b</text><text x='335' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>1  2</text><text x='335' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(1,2 has c1 and c2)</text><text x='200' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Query 1 and 2 both compute R ÷ S</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table R with columns a,b,c and table S with column c. R÷S returns (1,2) because it is the only (a,b) pair that appears with every c value in S.

given.aim: Verify that Query 1 (relational algebra) and Query 2 (TRC) both compute the relational division R ÷ S.

terms.meaning: R has attributes a, b, c and S has only attribute c. R-S = {a, b}.
terms.example: R stores triples, S stores single c values.
terms.connects: Division R÷S finds all (a,b) pairs in R that are paired with every c in S.

terms.term: Query 1: Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
terms.meaning: Standard relational algebra formula for division: R ÷ S.
terms.connects: This is the textbook RA formula for relational division.

terms.term: Query 2: { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) }
terms.meaning: TRC version: find (a,b) tuples such that there is no c in S for which (a,b,c) is absent from R.
terms.example: If (1,2) is in result, then for every c in S, (1,2,c) must be in R.
terms.connects: This is the standard TRC formula for relational division.

given.plan: Recognize both Query 1 and Query 2 as standard formulations of relational division R ÷ S. The RA formula for division is exactly Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R), and the TRC formula matches the logical definition of division. Both are equivalent.

steps.title: Identify Query 1 as relational division in RA
steps.formula_raw: R ÷ S = Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
steps.apply:
Query 1: Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
With R-S = {a,b} and S = {c}:
- Pi_{a,b}(R): all (a,b) pairs appearing in R
- Pi_{a,b}(R) x S: all combinations of (a,b) pairs with c values in S
- Pi_{a,b}(R) x S - R: combinations where (a,b,c) does NOT exist in R (missing pairs)
- Pi_{a,b}(Pi_{a,b}(R) x S - R): (a,b) pairs that are missing at least one c from S
- Subtracting: (a,b) pairs that are NOT missing any c — i.e., paired with ALL c in S
This is exactly R ÷ S.
steps.note: This is the standard textbook formula for relational division in relational algebra.

steps.title: Identify Query 2 as relational division in TRC
steps.formula_raw: R ÷ S = { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND for_all u in S: tu in R }
steps.apply:
Query 2: { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) }
Rewriting: 't is an (a,b) pair from R, and there does NOT exist a c value in S such that (a,b,c) is not in R'
Equivalently: 'for every c in S, (a,b,c) is in R'
This is the standard TRC formula for R ÷ S.

Both Query 1 and Query 2 compute R ÷ S, so they are equivalent.
steps.note: NOT (exists u: P(u)) is equivalent to for_all u: NOT P(u), confirming this is division.

questions.formula_note: Relational Division (R ÷ S): RA formula Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R); TRC formula { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT(tu in R)) }. Both are equivalent.
